what_it_does: This file is the front-end API client for the canvas application. It exports a single `api` object whose methods wrap `fetch` calls to the backend bridge, a `jr` helper that normalizes JSON responses and HTTP errors, and several pure helper functions for converting node config schemas into form fields, coercing config values, formatting relative time, and interpreting build-intent/decision items.
observations: A single `api` object exposes roughly 70 backend endpoints covering graph state, chat, voice, model loading, cognition, review/walkthrough, journeys, annotations, corpus query, and build-intent governance.
The `jr` helper checks `r.ok`, parses structured `{error}` bodies, and returns a normalized `{error}` object instead of throwing, so existing callers that branch on `r.error` become more robust.
`chatStream` and `voiceStream` use ndjson streaming; `chatStream` throws on `{type:error}` while `voiceStream` returns the raw `Response` for the caller to read.
`tts` deliberately does not use `jr` because it returns a `Blob`.
`voiceLog` is fire-and-forget and swallows any failure.
Config helpers (`configSchemaToFields`, `coerceConfigValue`, `resolveOptions`) turn backend registry schemas into inspector field definitions and coerce edited values back to numbers/null.
Build-intent helpers (`isBuildIntent`, `buildPhase`, `deriveOutcome`) interpret backend-owned payload/status fields for the wire UI.
Nearly every method and section is annotated with ticket codes (F5, D4, X16, G-4, etc.) and governance comments.
standouts: `jr` is the central error-normalization seam: it converts non-2xx HTTP responses into returned `{error}` objects, fixing a prior white-screen path where `api.chat` resolved to `{error}` and `sendChat` set `undefined` history.
The `api` object is a very large, flat registry of backend endpoints with inline semantic documentation tying each route to backend functions and governance rules.
The config-schema helpers enforce registry-is-truth: enum options come from `getMODEL_OPTIONS()` or inline schema fields, never hardcoded in this file.
conventions: Ticket-code comments prefix almost every method or block (e.g. F5, D4, X16, B1, L-fe).
Errors are normalized to returned objects rather than thrown rejections, matching existing caller patterns.
Streaming endpoints are explicitly separated from one-shot JSON endpoints.
Pure helper functions are exported individually and kept stateless.
Comments repeatedly invoke design principles: registry-is-truth, fail-loud, operator-only, reflects-never-owns, no parallel hardcoded copy.
concerns: `tts` bypasses `jr` and calls `r.blob()` unconditionally, so an HTTP error will resolve to a Blob containing an error page rather than a normalized `{error}` object.
`voiceLog` is intentionally fire-and-forget, which means trace writes can fail silently.
`chatStream` throws on stream errors while the rest of the API returns `{error}` objects, creating a mixed error-handling contract.
Most endpoint payloads and responses are typed as `any`, so the file documents contracts in comments rather than in TypeScript types.
notes: This is the front-end's single backend-communication module: it centralizes fetch wrappers, error normalization, and a handful of pure UI helpers, with heavy inline documentation mapping each route to backend features and governance invariants.
questions: What is the exact TypeScript contract for each backend endpoint's request/response shape?
How does the backend guarantee that `getMODEL_OPTIONS()` is kept in sync with the registry schemas it serves?
Why does `chatStream` throw instead of returning `{error}` like every other `api` method?
What consumer relies on `tts` returning a `Blob`, and how does it handle non-2xx responses?
purpose_vs_actual: The file's stated purpose is to be the API client plus pure registry/format helpers, and that matches what it does.
intention_signals: The file is trying to be a stable, self-documenting bridge between the React UI and the backend: it wants every backend interaction to go through one place, wants HTTP errors to become visible UI state instead of crashes, and wants UI helpers to derive their behavior from backend-owned data rather than hardcoded front-end assumptions.
novelty: The unusual aspect is the density of narrative comments that tie each fetch wrapper to specific backend functions, governance rules, and ticket codes, effectively making the API client a living index of the system's backend surface and invariants.
contribution: This file contributes a consolidated, comment-rich API surface, a reusable error-normalization helper that prevents white-screens, and pure helpers for schema-to-form conversion and build-intent interpretation that a fused system would keep as the canonical front-end/backend seam.
summary_for_embedding: A TypeScript API client module that wraps backend fetch calls with error normalization and provides pure helpers for config schema conversion, build-intent interpretation, and relative-time formatting.
intention_for_embedding: To centralize all front-end backend communication, normalize HTTP errors into returned error objects, and keep UI formatting helpers close to the API surface they consume.

symbols.does: Normalizes a fetch Response: returns parsed JSON on success, otherwise returns a structured `{error}` object.
symbols.description: `jr` checks `r.ok`, parses the JSON body on 2xx responses, and on non-2xx responses attempts to read a backend `{error}` body before falling back to `<status> <statusText>`. It returns errors rather than throwing so existing callers that branch on `r.error` continue to work.

symbols.does: Resolves the option list for a schema field from a registry key or inline array.
symbols.description: Given a field definition, `resolveOptions` first looks up `options_from` in `getMODEL_OPTIONS()`, then falls back to an inline `options` array, returning an empty array when neither is present. This keeps enum choices registry-driven.

symbols.does: Transforms a backend node config schema into flat inspector field definitions.
symbols.description: For each key in the schema it produces a field object with `key`, `label`, `type` (select for enums/options, input otherwise), `rawType`, `options`, and `default`, using `resolveOptions` to populate choices.

symbols.does: Coerces an edited form string back to the declared schema type.
symbols.description: For `number` fields it converts the string to a Number, preserving `null` for empty or invalid input; for all other fields it returns the raw string unchanged.

symbols.does: Formats an ISO timestamp as a short relative age string.
symbols.description: `relTime` returns 'now', 'Ns', 'Nm', or 'Nh' based on how many seconds/minutes/hours have elapsed since the given ISO time.

symbols.does: Discriminates a surfaced item as a build-intent by checking `payload.intent === 'build'`.
symbols.description: This helper reads the backend-owned discriminator and returns true only when the payload explicitly declares a build intent.

symbols.does: Maps a build-intent's backend status to an operator-legible phase label and CSS class.
symbols.description: `buildPhase` translates statuses like 'implemented', 'presented', and requeue/failure indicators into labels such as 'implemented ✓', 'dispatched · running…', 'surfaced back — needs you', or 'awaiting approval'.

symbols.does: Derives a plain-language outcome line and a demonstrable node-type from a build result.
symbols.description: `deriveOutcome` inspects `payload.build_result.changed_files` to detect a newly-added node type, returns a canvas-demonstrable instruction if that type is live, otherwise returns the result summary or a generic completion message.
